# CaveWhere User Manual

CaveWhere is a Qt/C++ desktop app for cave mapping and 3D visualization: it
morphs hand-drawn 2D survey sketches into 3D cave models, detects loop-closure
blunders, syncs projects between team members over Git, and imports LiDAR notes.
This is the machine-readable index of the user manual — read the linked pages to
answer user questions. Each entry is `- [Title](path): summary`.

This manual is being built out; pages are added chapter by chapter. Every page
here exists and is safe to read. See AUTHORING.md for the conventions all pages
follow.

## Start here

- [Why CaveWhere](concepts/why-cavewhere.md): The problems CaveWhere solves for cave surveyors (2D→3D morphing, loop closure, Git sync, LiDAR, keywords, project format) and how features map to them.
- [How a Project Is Organized](concepts/data-model.md): The data model as one nested tree — Region (the whole project, holds every cave) → Cave (one system; reports length/depth; the level a coordinate system is chosen on) → Trip (one team, one day, one set of instruments — the load-bearing level, because calibration and declination live here: everything measured that day needs the same correction; also owns the team, whose names become Caver keywords) → Survey chunk (a continuous, non-branching run of shots — a side passage starts its own) → Shot (a leg between two stations with distance/compass/clino — the skeleton) and, on the drawing branch off the trip, Note (a scan/PDF/SVG/LiDAR drawing) → Scrap (a digitized outline carpeted onto the survey; its plan/running/projected type lives here). Stations (named points like A1) thread both branches together and are matched by name across the whole cave, so consistent naming is what joins trips and anchors scraps. The key consequence: the survey is SOLVED, not just stored — fixing one shot re-runs the Survex network solve, so stations elsewhere move (loop closure), and because scraps are anchored to stations they re-carpet automatically to follow. Keywords and version history cut across the tree (keywords tag any level and drive layer visibility; a save versions the whole tree at once, so rollback moves everything together).
- [Directions and Coordinate Systems](concepts/coordinate-systems.md): The mental model behind declination, grid convergence, and georeferencing. THREE norths that don't coincide: magnetic (where the compass points; the pole moves year to year), true (the geographic pole), and grid ("up" on a projection's grid). A raw compass reading crosses all three. Magnetic→true is the declination (added to each bearing; per trip against the trip's date because it drifts; tens of degrees possible); getting it wrong leaves the map the right shape and length but rotated off true. True→grid is the grid convergence, which only appears once a cave is georeferenced (before that there's no projection); CaveWhere then applies (declination − grid convergence) automatically so corrected bearings land on grid north matching the projected coordinates — which is why a georeferenced cave's correction isn't simply its declination. Every cave starts LOCAL: internally consistent shape but floating, with absolute position and rotation unset. Georeferencing places it by FIXING one or more stations to real-world coordinates in a chosen coordinate system (= a datum, the Earth-shape model, PLUS a projection, how it's flattened to a grid). What it buys: overlay on surface maps/imagery, combining with georeferenced point clouds/LiDAR in one coordinate space, auto declination (needs the cave's location), and coordinates others can use. The task of georeferencing (fixing stations, choosing a CS, reading convergence) is its own not-yet-written chapter; this page is the concept behind it.
- [Glossary](concepts/glossary.md): Definitions of cave-surveying and CaveWhere terms — station, shot, LRUD, scrap, declination, grid convergence, loop closure, backsight, trip, lead, morphing.

## Chapters

- [Install CaveWhere](getting-started/install-cavewhere.md): Downloads for every platform are at https://cavewhere.com/downloads/. CaveWhere is a desktop app, free and open source — nothing to buy, no licence key, no account to register, and no network needed (syncing is opt-in). macOS gets a .dmg (signed and notarized, so it opens without a security warning); Windows an installer; Linux an AppImage (mark executable and run, no root, nothing to install). On macOS and Windows ONLY, installing registers .cw and .cwproj as CaveWhere files so a project opens by double-click — which matters because a directory project is a folder, and double-clicking the .cwproj inside it beats hunting through the Open dialog; those two also register the cavewhere:// link scheme, so a shared project link opens the app straight from the browser. NEITHER applies on Linux: the AppImage is a single file you run rather than something that installs itself, so nothing registers the file types, and there is no one way to register a file type across the desktop environments Linux users run. Nothing is out of reach on Linux, you just start from inside the app: a cave on disk via File → Open… or the recent projects list; a shared link via **File → Open from Link…**, pasting it in (the link is the same, you hand it to CaveWhere yourself instead of the browser doing it). Building from source is documented in the repo README and the GitHub Actions workflows; the one user-visible catch is that a source build can lack optional pieces the official packages always include — notably PDF support for notes, which needs Qt's PDF module at build time and is reported by Settings → PDF / SVG.
- [Set Up Your Identity](getting-started/set-up-your-identity.md): A fresh install opens on "Let's set you up!" and asks for a name and email before showing anything else — this is a gate, not a splash: there is no Skip and no project behind it. The reason is that every save is a version recorded in the project's history and a version records who made it, like signing a survey book; CaveWhere asks once up front rather than interrupting the first save. The two fields have no labels, only placeholders ("Your name", "your@email.com"). Next only proceeds when both are valid, otherwise it shakes and shows "No name found. Enter a name" or "Enter a valid Email address ex. your@email.com". Any name is accepted; the email is only checked for shape — CaveWhere never sends anything to it and never verifies it exists. The email is asked for because Git identifies an author by email, which is what lets a service like GitHub recognize a commit as yours. Nothing is sent anywhere: the identity is written into the project's history on your disk, and goes no further unless you opt into syncing. Stored ONCE PER MACHINE in CaveWhere's own settings, not inside any project, so every project on that machine is signed with it and it does not travel with a project you hand to someone else (their copy asks them to set up, and their saves carry their name). Change it later at Settings → Git under "Git Identity" (File menu → Settings…), same two fields, applied as you type with no Save button; editing changes how future saves are signed and never rewrites the author on versions already in the history.
- [Open the Example Cave](getting-started/open-the-example-cave.md): Phake Cave 3000 — the finished, multi-trip example project this manual's screenshots use — is opened from a SHARE LINK that downloads (git-clones) it: https://cavewhere.com/open?repo=https://github.com/Cavewhere/PhakeCave3000.git. On macOS and Windows you just CLICK the link ("Open the Example Cave") and the browser hands it to the app. On Linux, clicking doesn't reach CaveWhere; instead use File → Open from Link… (File is a sidebar button on Linux), paste the repo URL https://github.com/Cavewhere/PhakeCave3000.git, and click Open. Either path opens the same "Clone Repository" dialog: it names the repo (github.com / Cavewhere/PhakeCave3000), shows a Destination folder you can change (click the path) or leave at the default, and — because the repo is PUBLIC — needs NO sign-in (leave the Account picker alone; signing in is only for private team repos, in the Collaboration chapter). Click Clone & Open; a progress bar tracks the download and CaveWhere opens the cave, first asking what to do with the currently-open project (CaveWhere holds one project at a time). The cave lands as a normal editable project on disk; your edits stay local and don't reach the shared online copy unless you deliberately sync.
- [Find Your Way Around](getting-started/find-your-way-around.md): A tour of the window, which has three regions: the sidebar (left), the breadcrumb bar (top), and the page filling the rest. There is no single main screen — a cave is both a 3D model and a tree of caves/trips/shots/notes — so the sidebar picks what you're doing and the breadcrumb says where you are. Sidebar: View (the 3D model), Data (caves and trips: survey tables, calibration, notes, scraps), Map (composing a map image to export as PNG/PDF/SVG). Each button REMEMBERS where you were, so returning to Data lands on the trip you were editing rather than the top of the tree. Below the buttons is a job list (background work like recomputing scraps, with progress bars — CaveWhere never blocks you, so this is where you see whether it has caught up); at the bottom the Automatic Update checkbox (on by default), which keeps the model in step with your edits — see scraps/carpeting.md for what turning it off suspends (the survey solve and loop closure too, not just carpets). The File menu is in the macOS system menu bar on macOS, and a File button at the top of the sidebar on Windows and Linux. Breadcrumb: an address like "Source / Data / Cave=Phake Cave 3000 / Trip=Release 0.08 / Note=001", read outside-in; every crumb is a link that jumps to that ancestor. Back/forward arrows walk history like a browser's (for destinations the breadcrumb can't reach). A "..." button swaps the trail for an editable text field holding the raw address. The sync button sits at the far right; its tooltip always says what clicking it will do; it is opt-in and ignorable when working alone. CaveWhere opens on the View page. Responsive: below ~800px wide the sidebar shrinks to a narrower strip, keeping the same buttons with smaller icons and smaller labels (labels are not dropped); below ~500 it disappears entirely and View/Data/Map move to a hamburger (☰) in the breadcrumb bar. Each page adapts on its own and measures ITS OWN width, not the window's, so the sidebar's width counts against it: tables become lists (a cave's trip table collapses to one trip per row, sortable headers replaced by a sort dropdown plus asc/desc button; the cave's leads and fixed stations do the same) and the 3D view's View/Layers side panel becomes a drawer sliding over the model. At about 1200px of page width the Notes list inside the survey editor is replaced by a thumbnail strip beside the note itself. Nothing is removed at any size, only reached differently — if a control is missing, widen the window.
- [Save a Project](projects-and-files/save-a-project.md): What Save means in CaveWhere, which is not what it means elsewhere. Every edit is written to disk as you type it, so Save does not flush your data — it marks a version in the project's history that you can roll back to. Those versions are visible on the History page — right-click the sync button (top-right) and choose History… — which lists every save newest-first with author, date, and the exact files it changed; CaveWhere titles them all "Save from CaveWhere", so date and changed-file list are what distinguish them. Consequences: an unexpected shutdown costs the checkpoint rather than the work in a directory project, and Discard is a rollback to the last save (it undoes work already written), not merely "close without saving". The exception is a bundle (.cw), which is unpacked to a temp folder while open, so its Save is what repacks the file. A brand-new project already exists, already on disk, but in a temporary folder under a random friendly placeholder name (Misty Cavern, Thunder Ridge); the first save gives it a permanent home and a real name. CaveWhere asks before you quit or switch away from a temporary project and only discards one if you choose to — it does not decide for you. Covers the first save (Ctrl+S and Save As both open the same dialog: Project Name, which names the folders and appears only on a first save; the format picker; Location — a parent folder for Directory, a full path for Bundle; the red "A folder already exists" which blocks Save versus the orange "An existing bundle will be overwritten" which doesn't), later saves, and Save As on a saved project — which makes a copy, leaves the original at its last save, keeps working in the copy, and does NOT rename the project (rename by double-clicking the name on the Data page). Save As is also how you change format, since there is no Export: directory→bundle when a cave is finished and you want one file to send or archive, bundle→directory when a .cw someone sent you grows into real work and you no longer want every save recompressing the whole cave. Also the quit prompt (Discard/Cancel/Save; Save & Sync with a remote; Delete/Cancel/Save for a never-saved temporary project; no prompt when unmodified or empty) and read-only projects made by a newer CaveWhere, where a Read-only banner names the version needed and Save/Save As are disabled to avoid silently dropping data this version can't represent.
- [Choose a Project Format](projects-and-files/project-formats.md): Directory (.cwproj) versus bundle (.cw) — both hold the same data and the same full history, and Save As converts between them freely, so choose by the size and life of the project. Bundle for a small one-off cave (one cave, a trip or two, something you'll finish and file) and for handing any project to someone or archiving it: one portable file, nothing to leave behind. Directory for a big project — several caves, years of trips, a team syncing. The reason the line falls there: a bundle recompresses the WHOLE project on every save, so its cost scales with total size (unnoticeable on one cave, painful on a multi-cave project full of scans), while a directory only writes what changed and so doesn't care how big it gets. Directory is the default: edits are written to disk continuously, saves stay fast at any size, and the files are directly accessible. Bundle's trade: it is unpacked into a temp folder while open, so edits only reach the .cw when you Save. A bundle is a zipped directory project including .git, so bundles keep version history and can still sync — the two rows people expect to differ and don't. Documents the app's own comparison table (from the ⓘ button beside the format picker), the on-disk layout of a .cwproj (an extension-less outer folder that is a Git repo, containing <Name>.cwproj plus a data folder named after the project, notes stored as the original image files, point clouds under GIS Layers/, and .git/), the fact that all non-image files are indented JSON, and that renaming the project physically renames the data folder and descriptor but never the outer folder. Legacy .cw files (v6 and older) are a SQLite database, not a zip; opening one converts it automatically and silently, and the first save overwrites the original path with a modern bundle — same name, same survey, different container, and a one-way upgrade that an older CaveWhere can no longer open, so keep a copy if you still need it readable by an old version. A read-only legacy file becomes a temporary project instead and routes to Save As, leaving the original untouched. For implementer-level detail — the exact protobuf schema per entity, path/sanitization rules, version history and compatibility rules, bundling, and Git/LFS — this page points at the official spec at `docs/cavewhere-format-spec.md` (outside this manual); prefer that over the manual for any question about writing a tool that reads or writes CaveWhere projects.
- [Open a Project](projects-and-files/open-a-project.md): CaveWhere holds one project at a time, so New (Ctrl+N) and Open (Ctrl+O) both deal with the current project first. Open filters on "CaveWhere Project (*.cwproj *.cw)" and identifies the format by reading the file rather than trusting the extension — necessary because .cw means both a modern bundle and a legacy SQLite database. For a directory project you select the .cwproj file inside the folder, not the folder. The recent projects list lives on the Source page, reached by clicking Source in the breadcrumb above Data (there is no sidebar button for it); each row is a link labelled with the project's FILE name — extension and all, not the project name set on the Data page — with the file's full path beneath, and right-clicking the path offers Show in Finder / Show in Explorer. It self-maintains: entries are added when you open, save, or download a project, and dropped when the file goes missing. Its Add button repeats New Project / Open / Online Project. Open from Online (Ctrl+Shift+O) and Open from Link belong to the collaboration chapter. What happens to the open project: no prompt if unmodified or brand-new-and-empty; Discard/Cancel/Save (plus Save & Sync with a remote) for a saved project; Delete/Cancel/Save for a never-saved temporary project, where Delete throws the whole project away.
- [The 3D View](view-3d/the-3d-view.md): Navigate the 3D cave model — orbit/pan/zoom (turntable: right-drag orbit, left-drag pan, wheel zoom; one-finger drag orbit, pinch zoom/pan), aim the camera by exact angle in the View tab (Reset; Azimuth with N/E/S/W snaps + lock + Animate/Duration spin; Vertical Angle Plan=90°/Profile=0° + lock; Projection orthogonal↔perspective), read the compass rose and scale bar (scale bar shows in orthogonal only), and filter the scene with keyword layers (Layers tab). STATION LABELS: each station's name is drawn beside its point in the plot; they auto-DECLUTTER (overlapping/too-dense labels are dropped, on-screen ones kept stable, so not every station is labelled at once — zoom in for more) and are fixed screen size (don't shrink with distance); they ride the survey line, so hiding a trip's `Line Plot` in the Layers tab hides its station names too. There is NO normal show/hide control for labels (only a Debug-menu toggle). The Projection control is a two-way animated SWITCH (Orthogonal↔Perspective; the handle slides but always settles on one end, no in-between) and in perspective a Field of View setting appears — see perspective-and-field-of-view.md.
- [Focus the View with Keyword Layers](view-3d/layers-and-keywords.md): The 3D view's Layers tab filters the scene by KEYWORD so a big project shows only the part you're working in (one trip, one caver's passage, just the leads, only plan scraps). Keywords are auto-generated `key = value` tags CaveWhere attaches to everything it draws — NEVER user-typed — and every object inherits the tags of what it hangs under, so one plan scrap carries its own Type=Plan plus the Cave/Trip/Year/Date/Caver of its trip and cave and can be found by filtering any of them. Common keys: Type (values Plan, Running Profile, Projected Profile = the three scrap types; Line Plot = centerline+station labels; Lead; LAZ Layer = point clouds; LiDAR), Cave (name), Trip + Year + Date, Caver (one per team member — why consistent name spelling matters: Phil≠Philip to the filter). ENTRY: 3D view right-side panel, click the Layers tab (View is the other tab); on a narrow window a floating stacked-sheets "layers" button opens the same panel as a drawer. STRUCTURE = two nested combinators. A GROUP is one bordered box holding one or more COLUMNS. Each column has a key dropdown (defaults to Type) and below it that key's VALUES as checkboxes each with a count in parens — `Plan (4)`, `Line Plot (2)`. (1) WITHIN a box, columns combine with AND (an object must pass EVERY column) AND each new column filters ONLY the objects that survived the column to its LEFT = DRILL-DOWN: Type→tick Plan, then + adds a column grouped by Caver listing only cavers present among the plan scraps, tick one → that caver's plan passage; + a third on Year narrows to one season. The + (plus) button on a column adds an AND column to its right; the − (dash) button removes a column. AND columns start ALL-TICKED (they narrow — you untick to subtract/hide). (2) The "Also Include" button at the panel bottom adds a second box = an OR group: each box is evaluated independently against the whole scene and the boxes are UNIONED (show box-1's keep PLUS box-2's keep) — this is how you show two unrelated things at once (one caver's plan scraps AND all leads; two caves). A fresh Also-Include box starts with NOTHING ticked (opposite of the first box) because its job is to ADD things back — you tick what to pull in. So: AND across columns to narrow, OR across boxes to add. Value list extras: click a row to select, Shift/Ctrl-click multi-select then one checkbox toggle applies to all selected; right-click → "Select All"; objects lacking the chosen key fall into a catch-all value "Others" so nothing silently vanishes. Changes apply LIVE in the 3D view; nothing here edits survey data — it's pure visibility. Same `Type = LAZ Layer` filter is how you hide/show a point cloud, and `Type = Lead` isolates leads.
- [Perspective and Field of View](view-3d/perspective-and-field-of-view.md): The Field of View control in the 3D view's View tab that the-3d-view.md doesn't cover, plus the projection choice it sits under. (1) The Projection control is a two-way ANIMATED SWITCH between Orthogonal and Perspective — the handle slides but always SETTLES on one end (you can't hold it partway; there is no blend). Which mode you're in changes: the SCALE BAR shows in orthogonal only (perspective → no single frame-wide scale → bar hidden); MEASURING and MAP EXPORT assume orthogonal, so switch back for those. (2) FIELD OF VIEW (FOV) = the perspective lens angle in degrees, shown in the Projection group ONLY while perspective is on (hidden in orthogonal — no lens angle to set). Low FOV = narrow/telephoto = zooms in and FLATTENS depth (near/far sizes converge, approaching ortho); high FOV toward 180° = very wide, exaggerated depth, eventually FISH-EYE bulge; DEFAULT 55° is a good natural-eye value; valid range 0–180°. WHICH TO USE: orthogonal for anything that must be right (measure, export a map, compare trips at matched scale, Plan/Profile that reads like a printed survey); perspective ~55° for anything that must communicate (presenting to the team, a video fly-through, intuitive 3D bearings).
- [Organize Caves and Trips](survey-data/caves-and-trips.md): The Region → Cave → Trip structure and why the trip is the load-bearing unit — a trip is one team, one day, one set of instruments, which is exactly the scope that shares a calibration and a declination. Covers Add Cave and Add Trip (and Import Survex beside it), the cave's trip table (Name, Date, Stations, Length, and a Decl column reading auto or manual), editing the trip's name and date by double-clicking them in the trip header (names must be unique within the cave or the rename is silently rejected; the date is `yyyy-MM-dd`, defaults to today, keeps only the day, and drives auto declination), and the Team section's Name and free-text Role chips. A team member's name is not just documentation: it automatically becomes a **Caver** keyword on the trip, and keywords drive layer visibility in the 3D view, so the team list is what lets you show only the passage a given person surveyed — which is why names must be spelled consistently (Phil and Philip are two different cavers to the filter). Roles do not become keywords.
- [Enter Survey Data](survey-data/enter-survey-data.md): The survey table — station rows carry the name and LRUD, shot rows carry Distance, Compass and Vertical Angle (the clino) and straddle the two station rows they join, because a shot is measured between stations while LRUD is measured at one. Columns are Station, Distance, Compass, Vertical Angle, L, R, U, D; compass is 0–360 degrees, vertical angle −90 to 90 or the words up/down. Rows appear automatically as you type into the last one and empty trailing rows are trimmed; Space (or the "Press Space to add another data block" bar) starts a new block, needed because a block is a continuous run that cannot branch, so a side passage must start its own. Also covers Tab/arrow/Enter navigation, the Press Tab auto-name guess, case-insensitive station names (a1 and A1 are one station), right-click insert/remove with strike-through preview (removing a station always removes a shot too; a block never shrinks below two stations), Exclude Distance (marks a re-survey so its length isn't counted twice — the shot still positions stations), Total Length, and LRUD-only shots (an explicitly entered zero distance, not an empty one).
- [Calibrate the Instruments](survey-data/calibration.md): Per-trip corrections for the instruments a team carried, so different teams' data lines up and loops between them close. The rule is UncorrectedValue + Calibration = TrueValue — the calibration is *added* to what you read, so a tape 1 m short takes −1. Covers the Front Sights and Back Sights checkboxes (at least one is required; ticking both enables the foresight/backsight cross-check that warns beyond 2°), the Distance box's calibration and its Units dropdown (meters or feet only, per trip, applying to shot distances and LRUDs alike), the separate front and back compass/clino calibrations, and the Backwards Compass/Clino (front sights) and Corrected Compass/Clino (back sights) checkboxes — both meaning the reading points the opposite way to what its column implies, which CaveWhere fixes by subtracting 180° from a compass or multiplying a clino by −1. A trip that comes out mirrored or 180° out is usually one of those four boxes.
- [Set the Declination](survey-data/declination.md): Correct magnetic bearings to true north. Ignoring declination doesn't break anything visibly — the cave plots and loops close, the whole map is just rotated off true north, which surfaces when it won't overlay a surface map or when trips surveyed years apart disagree. The rule is MB + D = TB: declination is added to the magnetic bearing. Auto mode computes it from the IGRF magnetic model using the trip's date and the cave's location, so it needs a fixed station; without one the Auto/Manual selector isn't shown at all and the value is typed by hand. Manual is right when there's no fixed station, when reproducing someone else's reduction, or when the team corrected declination on the instrument in the cave (in which case enter 0, not the real declination). Imported declinations arrive as Manual on purpose. Explains both warnings: a trip with no date, and a manual value that differs from the computed one by half a degree or more.
- [Fix Survey Errors](survey-data/survey-errors.md): What CaveWhere's per-cell error badges mean. Errors (fatal) stop the cave plotting; warnings don't and can be suppressed; the trip header shows a running "There are n errors/warnings" and hides when clean. Fatal: Missing station name; Duplicate station name (only ever the *immediately previous* station — a name repeating elsewhere is how loops close, so only a shot from A3 to A3 is wrong); a missing distance; a missing compass/clino when both it and its counterpart are empty; a non-numeric LRUD; and mixing up/down with numbers across a shot's foresight and backsight. Warning: Frontsight and backsight differs by n° — the 2° cross-check, whose usual causes are a transposed digit, iron near the shot, or a wrong corrected/backwards box (about 180° out means a convention problem); and a missing LRUD. Also covers the unconnected-survey-leg solve failure, which is nearly always a station name that doesn't match the one it should tie to (case is never the cause — CaveWhere ignores it).
- [Check Loop Closure and Find Blunders](loop-closure/check-loop-closure.md): Where loop closure surfaces and how to hunt a blunder with it. A loop = a route that returns to a station it already visited; the two paths never meet exactly, and the gap (MISCLOSURE) is your best cross-check on the data — small = ordinary instrument noise, large = a BLUNDER (transposed digit, compass read backwards, mistyped distance). CaveWhere runs loop closure AUTOMATICALLY: it embeds Survex, whose `cavern` solver does a least-squares network adjustment on every survey edit, spreading misclosure across the whole loop (weighted per leg) to place every station — that IS the adjusted 3D line plot. Fixing a blunder is self-correcting: change the reading → network re-solves → loop closes → station-anchored scraps re-carpet. The one dedicated UI is the CAVERN OUTPUT page (Data page → region ⋯ menu → "Cavern Output"; also File menu → "Cavern Output…"; the item reads "Cavern Output (solve error)" if the last solve failed). It has: a status line ("Last solve completed successfully" / red "Cavern reported an error during the last solve" + message), a Solve button (forces a re-solve; rarely needed since it's automatic), a "Cavern log" tab (Survex's plain-text summary — the key line is "There are N loops", plus station/shot counts and total+adjusted length), and a "Loop closure" tab (the per-loop error report, ENABLED ONLY when the cave has ≥1 loop — greyed out otherwise). The loop-closure report gives one block per loop: first line lists the loop's stations; then "Original length L m (N legs), moved M m (m/leg). Error E%" where MOVED is the misclosure distance and ERROR% (misclosure as % of loop length) is the headline number to scan (tenths of a % = healthy, a couple % + = a leg is wrong); then an overall standardised-error value and "H: h V: v" (actual error ÷ the error the instruments alone should give — ~1 is fine, well above 1 means worse than the gear explains → blunder; high H = compass/distance, high V = clino). GENERAL Error% GUIDELINES (rough, instrument/length-dependent, not pass/fail): GOOD < ~0.5% (tight; digital/DistoX routinely better), OKAY ~0.5–2% (fine for longer loops or compass-clino-tape; adjustment absorbs it), BAD > ~2% and near-certain blunder past ~5%. CAVEAT: Error% is length-dependent — a SHORT loop shows an alarming % from an ordinary absolute error, so also check the "moved" distance; the E and H:V ratios are NOT length-fooled (E near 1 = closed as well as instruments allow even if % looks high), so when % and E disagree trust E. Finding the bad leg: the report names the LOOP, not the leg — so (1) check the front/back-sight tolerance WARNING first ("Frontsight and backsight differs by x°", in the survey table — see survey-errors.md), (2) walk the loop's legs for a transposed digit / compass ~180° off / mistyped distance / stray up-down, and (3) suspect a TIE-IN ERROR (a bad connection, not a bad reading: two runs joined at the wrong station via a mistyped/reused name — measurements fine, topology wrong). Technique to find a tie-in error: BREAK THE LOOP on purpose by adding a char (e.g. "x") to one tie-in station's name so it stops matching its counterpart — the survey stays connected through the loop's other end so it still plots, and the freed end floats to where ITS OWN shots put it; view that free end in the 3D viewer — it lands nearest the station it SHOULD tie to (obvious when the loop's error is low), so rename it to that. If the free end lands back on the name it already had, the tie-in was fine and the blunder is a reading. IMPORTANT HONESTY POINT: a large closure error does NOT put a red/yellow badge on any survey cell — those cell badges are DATA checks (missing reading, name won't connect), a SEPARATE system from loop closure; the misclosure lives only in the Cavern Output report. "There are 0 loops" is normal, not an error — a linear/out-and-back survey is a tree with nothing to close; loops appear when passages reconnect or a new trip ties back onto an existing station. The example cave (Phake Cave 3000) is loop-free (14 stations, 13 shots, 0 loops), so the screenshot shows the Cavern log with "There are 0 loops" and a correctly-disabled Loop closure tab. Automatic Update off suspends the solve so loop closure stops running (see scraps/carpeting.md).
- [Georeference a Cave](georeferencing/georeference-a-cave.md): Place a floating survey on the real-world map. An un-georeferenced cave has the right shape but no place — every one is anchored at the SAME local origin, so adding a second cave piles them on top of each other in the 3D view, and auto declination stays unavailable (it needs a location). Georeferencing is TWO steps on TWO pages: (1) choose the PROJECT COORDINATE SYSTEM on the Data page's Geospatial box — Local (the default, = not georeferenced), UTM (pick zone 1–60 + N/S hemisphere), or Custom… (search the full EPSG catalog); there is deliberately NO Lat/Lon option because Survex's cavern can't solve in a geographic system, it needs a projected metric grid. (2) Fix at least one station: open the cave page, click the Fix stations link (shows the count, 0 when not georeferenced) → Fix Stations page → Add Fix → a row with Station (must match a real survey station), Input CS (the system the typed numbers are in — INDEPENDENT of the project CS; set it to Lat/Lon (WGS84) to type GPS lat/lon even in a UTM project and CaveWhere converts), and Easting/Northing/Elevation in meters. Double-click a field to edit; right-click a row to remove; you can fix more than one station and the network ties to all of them. Fixing is what MOVES the cave: it re-solves anchored to real coordinates instead of the local origin, so multiple caves separate into true relative positions, grid convergence starts reporting, and auto declination unlocks. CaveWhere keeps a WORLD ORIGIN (an offset near the survey so the scene stays centered even at large coordinates), recomputed automatically on every solve and never stored (there's a manual "Recenter world origin" on the Data page region menu). Once georeferenced, the 3D view's coordinate picker reads any point back out in the project CS, WGS84 lat/lon, and elevation, each with Copy.
- [Understand Grid Convergence](georeferencing/grid-convergence.md): The per-cave readout that appears once a cave is georeferenced, next to the Fix stations link on the cave page. Grid convergence = the angle between TRUE north (the geographic pole) and GRID north (the projection grid's "up"); a projection lines up with true north on only one line and leans away east/west of it, so convergence depends on projection AND location (within one UTM zone it can vary a degree+ from central meridian to edge). It does NOT drift with time (no magnetic component) and is computed at the cave's first fixed station. Readings: "0.74° at a1" (the value being applied, at that station; hover shows the CS); "n/a (no fix station)" (has a CS but no fix); "n/a (no coordinate system)" (has a fix but no project CS); "n/a (geographic CS)" (a lat/lon CS has no grid, so convergence is 0). The two missing-piece "n/a"s are a checklist — georeferencing needs BOTH a CS and a fix. Why it matters in practice (two payoff cases): (1) FIXING MORE THAN ONE STATION — the fixed coordinates live on the grid so the direction between them is a grid bearing, but the survey measures a true bearing (magnetic+declination); the gap = the convergence, and leaving it in means the traverse can't reach both fixes at once, surfacing as a loop-closure error between them that re-measuring won't clear; correcting for convergence puts measured directions in the fixes' grid so the loop closes. (2) ALIGNING WITH AERIAL LIDAR / A POINT CLOUD — those arrive already in a projected grid oriented to grid north, so a cave turned only to true north sits rotated off them by the convergence (error grows with distance from the entrance); turning the cave onto grid north lines it up with the scan. Same underlying reason: anything already on the grid (GPS control, aerial LiDAR, surface map) speaks grid north, so the cave must too. Why it matters even if you never read it: when cavern solves it produces a GRID bearing in two steps: (1) correct to true north, magnetic + declination = true bearing; (2) correct for the grid, true bearing − grid convergence = grid bearing. Chained: grid bearing = magnetic reading + declination − grid convergence. The endpoint is a grid bearing (not a true one), matching the projection grid. On a local cave convergence is 0 and the correction is just declination; georeferencing switches the second term on automatically, which is why a placed cave's total bearing correction is NOT simply its declination. KEY FRAMING: "the grid" is not abstract — it IS the project coordinate system the user chose (the UTM zone/projection), and that same system is the frame CaveWhere lays the 3D model out in and exports to; in plan view, north on screen IS grid north and the model's eastings/northings are the grid's, so the convergence correction aligns the survey to the map CaveWhere already draws (a local cave is drawn to true north instead). Illustration (hand-authored SVG, after a diagram by Mike Futrell): two points at longitude 108°E on one true-north meridian get different UTM eastings because grid north leans from true north.
- [Add a Point Cloud](point-clouds/add-a-point-cloud.md): Bring an aerial or surface LiDAR scan into the project as a geospatial layer so the cave is drawn inside its real terrain (relate passages to surface sinks/cliffs/buildings, plan digs, present in context). A point cloud = millions of measured 3D terrain points in .laz (compressed) or .las (uncompressed); CaveWhere reads either. PREREQUISITE: the cave and the cloud must share one real-world grid, so the cave must be GEOREFERENCED (or become so as you add the cloud). WHERE: point clouds are PROJECT-wide, not per-cave — Data page → Geospatial box (which also holds the project Coordinate system control) → Layers link (shows the count, 0 when none) → Geospatial Layers page. ADD: "Add LAZ Files" button → file picker filtered to "LAZ point clouds (*.laz *.las)", multi-select. CaveWhere COPIES each file into the project's GIS Layers folder so it travels with the project; large scans stream/load in the background with a progress entry. TABLE columns: Name (the file's name — rename the file before importing to label it), Coordinate System (the layer's OWN grid read from the file, e.g. "NAD83 / UTM zone 13N"; hover for the full definition; needn't match the project's), Points (count). COORDINATE SYSTEMS / how it lines up: a LiDAR file normally carries its own CS; CaveWhere REPROJECTS every point into the PROJECT CS so cloud + survey overlay — no manual alignment. Two cases: (1) project has no CS yet → adding the first cloud ADOPTS that cloud's CS as the project's and centers the project on it (quick path: drop an aerial scan, project takes its grid); (2) the cloud has no embedded CS (some .las) → nothing to reproject from, help box "One or more layers don't have an embedded coordinate system. Set the project's coordinate system on the Data page to align them with surveys." → set the project CS to the one the cloud's numbers are in. VISIBILITY vs ARCHIVE vs REMOVE (distinct): to HIDE a cloud in the 3D view use the LAYERS tab keyword filter (same as caves/trips) — every cloud is tagged Type:"LAZ Layer" so grouping by Type gives a "LAZ Layer" group to tick/untick; there is NO per-row visibility checkbox on the Geospatial Layers page. RIGHT-CLICK a row → Disable/Enable = UNLOAD not hide (setEnabled(false) frees the geometry and reload() early-returns, so a disabled layer isn't drawn AND isn't in memory) → this is for ARCHIVING a cloud you're done with and to keep a big scan from spending load time on every open; it stays listed, dimmed, marked "Disabled"; the clip tool auto-Disables its source clouds. Remove = confirm then delete the COPIED file from GIS Layers (original untouched). IN THE 3D VIEW: (a) the cloud reads as a LIT SURFACE not a dot field because CaveWhere shades it with Eye-Dome Lighting (EDL) — each point darkened by how far its on-screen neighbours sit in front/behind, bringing out relief/edges; ALWAYS ON, no toggle in the shipping UI; (b) point size defaults from the scan's mean spacing so neighbours just touch (gap-free, lets the shading read as a surface) but IS TUNABLE — hover the 3D view, HOLD "P" and scroll the wheel/trackpad (re-routes the wheel from camera zoom to the LAZ scene node's worldRadius; the key is P not R). There is no point-size SLIDER, but the hold-P+wheel gesture is the control.
- [Clip a Point Cloud](point-clouds/clip-a-point-cloud.md): Draw a polygon in the 3D view to KEEP or REMOVE the points inside it, writing the result to a new, smaller layer. WHY: an aerial scan covers a whole area (square km, tens of millions of points) when you want only the strip over the cave — the full cloud makes the view heavy and the project large. WHERE: 3D view bottom toolbar → Clip (scissors) button, tooltip "Clip point cloud" (alongside Pick and Measure). It acts on the point-cloud layers currently SHOWN in the view (render-visible = enabled AND keyword-visible), so to clip just one of several, hide the others via the Layers-tab keyword filter (or Disable them on the Geospatial Layers page). DRAW: the polygon is a shape on SCREEN; CaveWhere EXTRUDES it straight back along the current line of sight into a CLIP VOLUME (a prism through the whole cloud) — a point is in/out by whether it falls inside the outline AS SEEN FROM THE CURRENT ANGLE, at any depth (NOT projected onto a ground plane: vertices land on the near plane via screenToWorldXYZ, containment is a 2D point-in-polygon test in EYE-XY with eye-Z IGNORED — projectPolygonToEyeXY, cwLazClipOperation.h:37-41). So the viewpoint IS the clip: look down = plan/footprint clip, look from the side = profile slice. That's why rotation/tilt is LOCKED while drawing (a swing would change what's inside). ORIENT FIRST, then pick up the tool. Click to drop each corner (≥3; help "Click to add more vertices. 3 minimum."), then close by clicking near the first corner (shows a snap indicator) or double-clicking ("Choose Crop or Erase, or cancel."). Esc cancels. APPLY: Crop = keep points INSIDE the polygon (common case — box the cave, crop the scan to it); Erase = remove points inside (cut away a building/spoil heap); Cancel = discard. OUTPUT (non-obvious): clipping does NOT edit the source — it writes a NEW layer named clip_1, clip_2, … holding the kept points, then AUTOMATICALLY DISABLES the source layer(s) so only the clip is drawn (originals stay in the project, dimmed/"Disabled"; Enable to bring back or Remove once happy). The new layer carries over EVERY point attribute (intensity, color, GPS time, classification) and is stamped with the project coordinate system; multiple visible layers are MERGED into one clip. Reads the source files FROM DISK point-by-point (not from memory), so it works on a cloud far too large to load at once.
- [Measure Distance and Bearing](measurement/measure-distance-and-bearing.md): Measure the straight-line distance, azimuth, and inclination between two points in the 3D view — a scratch-pad reading taken off the finished model, NOT survey data (it lives only while shown, a new pair replaces it, nothing is saved and it never changes the survey). WHERE: 3D-view bottom-left floating toolbar → Measure (ruler icon, tooltip "Measure distance and bearing"), beside the coordinate Pick and the Clip tools; click again to turn off. You can still orbit/pan/zoom while measuring (left-click is reserved for placing points; right-drag + scroll still work — rotation is NOT locked, unlike the clip tool). TWO-POINT flow: hover shows a preview marker + running distance on the connecting line; click point A ("Click to place the first point."), move for a live A→cursor preview ("Click to place the second point."), click point B to freeze the readout ("Click to start a new measurement, or press Esc to exit."). Click again = restart; Esc = exit; only one measurement at a time. SNAPPING: by default (Free placement) a click lands wherever the line of sight meets the model (wall, point cloud, anywhere); near a survey STATION on the centerline the marker becomes a ring and the point snaps EXACTLY to that station. A point behind geometry can't be snapped (the wall is hit first) — orbit until it's visible. "Station only" switch (in the readout) makes clicks place ONLY when snapped to a station (no-op over open passage) — use it for a true station-to-station distance. READOUT panel (top-right, titled "Measurement"): Distance group = Straight-line (3D) + Horizontal (2D, height ignored); Direction group = Azimuth (0–360°) + Inclination (−90°..+90°, 0=level); By Axis group = signed Easting (X) / Northing (Y) / Vertical (Z) (sign carries direction — negative vertical = second point lower, negative easting = west). Header UNIT selector switches all lengths at once between metres/kilometres/feet/miles (default metres, remembered); every value is selectable text; Copy button puts the whole readout on the clipboard as text; on a narrow view the panel collapses to just Distance with an Expand button. AZIMUTH NORTH REFERENCE (the key control): a selector beside the Azimuth row + a "?" help button, three options in AzimuthReference enum order — Grid / True / Magnetic (today). Mnemonic "Grid = the map, True = the globe, Magnetic = your compass". Grid (default, always available) = north of the coordinate grid the model is drawn in (matches the 3D view, the survey coordinates, a printed map). True = geographic north; differs from grid by the GRID CONVERGENCE at this location. Magnetic (today) = where a compass points now; differs from true by the magnetic DECLINATION, which drifts, so it's computed for TODAY's date (a live reading, not stored). True + Magnetic REQUIRE the cave GEOREFERENCED (need a real position + coordinate system) — on a local/un-georeferenced cave they're greyed out and only Grid is offered (there grid north is just the model's own +Y axis). Hover the azimuth value for the exact convergence/declination applied; a set-but-unresolvable CS shows "n/a" with a tooltip reason. Math (cwMeasurementMath::between + cwAzimuthReference::resolve): scene axes +X East/+Y North/+Z up, gridAzimuth=atan2(dx,dy) wrapped [0,360); True=grid+convergence (cwGridConvergence), Magnetic=grid+convergence−declination (cwDeclination/IGRF, east-positive).
- [Add Notes to a Trip](notes/add-a-note.md): Import the drawings made underground as notes on a trip, however they were recorded — scanned paper, a PDF, a digital sketch exported as SVG from an app like TopoDroid, or a phone LiDAR scan; they all import the same way. Covers Add → Notes or 3D Model (reachable from the + beside the Notes heading or the Add button in the note toolbar), the file types accepted (png, jpg, tif, bmp, gif, webp, svg, plus pdf — the official builds include PDF support, and it is only missing from a build compiled from source without Qt's PDF module, which Settings → PDF / SVG reports, and glb for LiDAR), one note per PDF page, PDF/SVG rasterization at the Settings resolution (96 ppi default), what import does to the file (copies the original into the project, applies EXIF orientation, caps very large images), and rotating (display only), removing, and revealing a note. There is no drag-and-drop.
- [Set the Image Resolution](notes/note-resolution.md): A note's DPI — why pixels need a real paper size before a scrap's "On Paper = In Cave" scale means anything, where the Image Info panel lives (Carpet mode only), measuring the resolution by clicking two points and giving their length on the paper, propagating one resolution to every note in the trip, resetting to the file's original, and what DPI does and doesn't change (it cancels out of an auto-calculated carpet but is load-bearing for a hand-typed scale). Mostly a raster concern: a PDF or SVG states its own page size so its resolution is computed, and a LiDAR note has no DPI at all. Fixes the "Invalid DPI" and "Invalid scale (check DPI)" errors.
- [Work with LiDAR Notes](notes/lidar-notes.md): Import a .glb scan as a note and georeference it. A scan does not replace sketching and won't out-draw a good sketcher. It records surfaces, and records them very well — better than anyone would draw a breakdown pile — but it cannot record what isn't a surface: airflow, which way on is worth pushing, where the water goes, or which features matter. A sketch is a set of judgements about exactly those things; a scan makes none. What a scan adds is a second dimension of survey data next to the sketch, useful for checking a sketch against the passage after the trip and for teaching a new sketcher. CaveWhere opens any polygonal glTF (.glb), not just LiDAR; PolyCam is the best-supported source and its scans arrive already up and already life-size, while photogrammetry models often come in with the wrong up and usually need their scale set by hand (photographs carry shape but not size). Order matters: set up first (+Y is up (PolyCam) is the default; Custom reveals an arrow tool), then north (auto-calculated from two or more stations, or set by tool/angle), then scale (normally 1:1; the row reads In Model, and a LiDAR note has no DPI). Place name-matched stations to tie the scan to the survey. LiDAR notes have no scraps, no leads, and no Rotate.
- [Scraps and Carpeting](scraps/carpeting.md): What a scrap is and how carpeting morphs a flat passage sketch onto the 3D survey (scale→grid→morph), why it re-drapes automatically when the survey changes, and the Compute Scraps / Scraps Visible debug controls.
- [Digitize a Scrap](scraps/digitize-a-scrap.md): Enter Carpet mode, trace a passage outline on a note by clicking points, place the survey stations inside it that anchor the scrap to the 3D survey, and mark leads (unexplored "go" passages) that morph onto the survey and collect on the cave's Leads page.
- [Choose the Scrap Type](scraps/scrap-types.md): The views surveyors draw (plan, projected profile, running profile, cross-section) illustrated from one passage, and how to pick the matching scrap type — plan (looking down), running profile (horizontal passage, wraps corners, keeps shot length), or projected profile (vertical shafts, one plane, foreshortens); cross-sections are drawn as projected profiles set by hand. Also the Scrap Info panel's other controls: Auto Calculate (derives values from stations and makes the rest read-only), north/up orientation, scale (On Paper = In Cave, computed from the note's DPI, with the 1:1 and unit-mismatch warnings), and the projected-profile-only azimuth.
- [Troubleshoot the Carpet](scraps/troubleshoot-carpeting.md): Why a carpet distorts and how to fix it — wrong scale (thin/fat) or rotation, mislabeled stations matched across the whole cave, wrong scrap type, plan vertical bumps, running-profile creases/seesaw, and splitting a sketch into smaller scraps.
- [Track and Export Leads](leads/track-and-export-leads.md): The per-CAVE Leads page gathers every lead (unexplored "go" passage) marked across the cave's scraps into one list, so the scattered question marks become a ranked, exportable to-do list for the next trip. Leads are CREATED during carpeting (digitize-a-scrap), not here; the empty page says "There's no leads. Add them in during carpeting". WHERE: the cave page's "Leads" link (shows the lead count) → the Leads page. COLUMNS: Done (a read-only check mark — you set done-state in the lead's editor, NOT in the list), Goto (jumps to the lead in the 3D view), Nearest (the auto-computed nearest survey station — where the lead sits in the cave), Size (width × height you estimated; an un-estimated dimension reads as -1), Distance (line-of-sight from a station YOU pick), Trip (a link that opens the scrap the lead was drawn on), Description. Click a header to SORT (wide layout); a narrow window becomes one lead per row with a Sort dropdown + asc/desc button. FILTER by description substring (case-insensitive). RANK BY DISTANCE: set "Lead Distance from" to a station (entrance, camp, wherever the next trip will be) and every lead's Distance becomes its straight-line (line-of-sight, through rock — not passage length) distance from it; sort by Distance to order by nearness; 0 m until a station is picked; the station name also labels the CSV distance column. LEADS IN 3D: because each lead is anchored in a scrap, carpeting morphs it onto the survey, so it appears in the 3D view as a QUESTION-MARK marker at its real position; Goto (or clicking a marker) centers the camera and opens a small Lead popup (with "Open in Notes" back to the drawing); leads show/hide as a group via the 3D view's layers. EDIT / MARK DONE: a lead's description, size, and DONE state are edited in the lead's popup (3D marker → Edit) or on the note in Carpet mode (Lead Info panel) — NOT in the list (its Done column only displays). The edit form has Completed (tick once pushed → the marker hides from the 3D view so only open leads clutter it, and a check mark appears in the list; untick if it still goes), Size (width/height + unit; blank if unknown), Description (free text). All three surfaces read/write the same lead. EXPORT CSV: the "Export CSV" button (enabled when there are leads) → file dialog → a .csv with one row per lead, columns Completed (Yes/No), Nearest Station, Trip, Size Width, Size Height, Size Units, "Distance to <station> (m)" (headed with the reference station, or "Distance to Reference Station" if none), Description. Rows come out in the list's CURRENT filter + sort order, so filter/sort first and the CSV lands trimmed and ranked; the export runs as a background "Export Leads CSV" job. Use it as a trip-planning list in the field or in a spreadsheet.
- [Tune the Warping Settings](scraps/warping-settings.md): Adjust grid resolution (grid spacing over the scrap), shot interpolation spacing (dummy control points added along each shot), max closest stations (how many nearest stations warp each point — counted over the interpolated dummy stations too, so with the default 0.5 m spacing ten points reach about 5 m of survey line; the two settings multiply into that reach), and smoothing radius (Gaussian average of nearby heights; never applied to running profiles) to trade carpet detail against performance and smoothness; each has a diagram. These settings are global, not per-project.
- [Import Surveys from Other Programs](import-export/import-surveys.md): Import always ADDS to the open project as new caves and never replaces it; a name that collides is renamed, not merged. On the Data page's Import button (desktop-only, hidden on mobile, beside Export): Survex (.svx) and Walls (.wpj / .srv, .srv multi-select) open a WIZARD showing the survey tree where you set each block to Don't Import / is a Cave / is a Trip (Import stays disabled until at least one is a Cave or Trip); Compass (.dat) imports directly with no wizard and shows a "Compass import" job in the sidebar; CSV (.csv) opens its own importer page. Survex/Walls show a parse-progress window and tabbed Survex/Walls Errors + Import Errors; Compass problems are warnings (you get the readable caves plus a dialog naming the file and line). Separately, a cave page's "Import Survex" button (always available, NOT desktop-gated) beside Add Trip imports one .svx as a new TRIP in that cave — date/team/calibration from the file, all shots flattened into the one trip — with no wizard; this is the usual way to bring in a centerline exported from a phone survey app like TopoDroid.
- [Import a CSV or Spreadsheet](import-export/import-csv.md): The CSV importer (Data → Import → CSV) reads a plain table and imports it as a new cave; the work is telling CaveWhere what each column means. Accepts .csv and .txt with any separator (comma default). Drag meanings between Available Columns and Used Columns and reorder Used Columns to match the file's column order; starting used columns are From/To/Length/Compass/Clino, available are Compass Backsight/Clino Backsight/Left/Right/Up/Down/Skip; Skip is the only meaning usable more than once (for columns to ignore). Options: Skip header lines (default 1), Separator (comma default), Length Unit (meters default; labels the numbers, doesn't convert), "Empty lines create new Trips" (off), "Associate LRUDs with" From station (default) or To station. A live CSV Text view (raw, numbered) and Preview table (parsed, More/Less toggles 20 rows vs all) confirm the mapping; a Status box reads Success or lists errors/warnings; the Import button is DISABLED while there are fatal errors (e.g. "Looking for N columns but found M on line L"), warnings don't block.
- [Export Surveys to Other Programs](import-export/export-surveys.md): The Export button (Data page and cave pages, desktop-only, beside Import; NOT in the File menu) writes survey data out; the project is untouched and there is no confirmation dialog or sidebar job — the file just appears. Survex (.svx): trip / cave / region (all caves), highest fidelity — shots (fore/back/both, plumbs as UP/DOWN), calibration, declination (*declination auto or the manual value), team (only Survex-recognized roles survive), LRUD, dates, and a duplicate flag on length-excluded shots; only the REGION export writes the output coordinate system (*cs), so a single-cave/trip .svx lacks it. Compass (.dat, cave only) UPPERCASES every station name because Compass is case-sensitive and CaveWhere is not (a1 → A1), so a round-trip does NOT preserve casing (re-import gives A1); also truncates names to 12 chars, strips spaces from the survey name, and writes only the .dat (no .mak). Chipdata (cave only) is leanest: 5-char station names, NO declination/calibration written, and the dialog adds no extension. To move a whole project between CaveWhere's own formats use Save As, not export.
- [Export a Map](import-export/export-a-map.md): The Map page (sidebar → Map) composes a printable picture of the cave — a map at a real paper scale — as opposed to survey data. A map is one or more LAYERS (rectangular captures of the 3D view, frozen at the camera angle they were taken from) arranged on a paper sheet, so aim the 3D view first (plan = straight-down orthographic, profile = side-on). Add Layer switches to the View page with a selection tool ("Select the area to add a map layer") — click-drag a rectangle, click Done, and the capture lands on the paper. Per-layer: Scale (On Paper = In Cave, shown "1:N" — the number that makes it a map), Size/Position/Rotation (also draggable in the preview), Scale Bar (on by default), Leads (off). Paper Size: Letter/Legal/A4/Custom, Portrait/Landscape, per-edge margins, and Resolution 100–600 DPI (default 300). Export to PNG (lossless, transparent), JPG (smaller, white bg, lossy), TIF (lossless, transparent), SVG (scalable vector), or PDF (print-ready vector page); the finished file opens in the system viewer. **In practice most cavers export SVG or PDF and redraw the map in a vector editor (Inkscape, Adobe Illustrator)** — CaveWhere lays the cave out to scale and the vector program is where the finished cartography happens; the raster formats are for when you want a finished image directly.
- [How Collaboration Works](collaboration/how-sync-works.md): The concept page for the Collaboration chapter — read before the how-tos. Sync lets a team work on the same cave without emailing files around or overwriting each other. Under the hood it is Git hosted on GitHub, but you press ONE Sync button — no branches, commands, or merge conflicts to resolve by hand. The shared copy on GitHub is the REMOTE; each person keeps their own LOCAL copy they actually edit; syncing only ever moves changes between your local copy and that one remote, never directly between two people (GitHub in the middle is what lets people never online at the same time collaborate). SAVE marks a version in your local history (does not touch the remote); SYNC pushes your saved versions up and pulls teammates' down — you can save many times between syncs and one sync carries them all. The merge is DATA-MODEL-AWARE: a 3-way merge that understands caves, trips, shots, notes, scraps, teams, LiDAR, so two people editing different parts of the cave merge cleanly with nothing to resolve. This is why the directory .cwproj format stores data as many small human-readable files (clean merge seams); bundled .cw can sync too. Sync is fully OPT-IN — no GitHub account or network is needed to use CaveWhere; ignore this chapter if you work alone.
- [Sign In to GitHub](collaboration/sign-in-to-github.md): To sync, connect CaveWhere to GitHub ONCE. This is SEPARATE from your IDENTITY (the name+email from Set Up Your Identity, stored per machine, which SIGNS your saves) — the GitHub sign-in AUTHORIZES the push; you can have an identity set and simply not be able to sync until you sign in. Uses GitHub's DEVICE FLOW: click "Connect to GitHub" → CaveWhere shows a short code ("Enter the code below at GitHub to finish signing in") → "Copy and Open GitHub" copies it and opens the browser → paste + approve on github.com → CaveWhere is polling and notices. No password is typed into CaveWhere, no SSH keys, NO personal access token. The first sign-in usually also requires INSTALLING the CaveWhere GitHub APP ("You're signed in, but CaveWhere isn't installed on any of your GitHub accounts yet") — installing is what grants access to the specific repos you choose. There is NO dedicated sign-in page; the panel appears in context (Sync button "Log in", File → Open from Online…, the Set Up Remote wizard, and the Reconnect popup). MULTIPLE accounts are supported, each shown as "GitHub (username)" in an Account picker with an "Add Account" entry; one is active at a time. The token is stored in the OS KEYCHAIN (service "CaveWhere"), one entry per account, NEVER in the project file, so handing someone a project never hands them your GitHub access; tokens refresh automatically and can expire → "GitHub Access Expired" → Reconnect. "Log out"/"Forget Account" removes the account from THIS DEVICE only (deletes the keychain token), does not touch GitHub, and does not remove a project's remote. PUBLIC-repo clone needs NO sign-in; pushing, private repos, browsing your own repo list, and creating a repo all require it.
- [Share a Project](collaboration/share-a-project.md): Two steps — give the project a REMOTE, then send a LINK. Start the SET UP REMOTE wizard from the Sync button ("Set up remote…" or clicking its upload-cloud icon), Remote Settings → "Add Remote", or the Share dialog's "Add a remote repository to enable sharing" link. Default path CREATE REPOSITORY: CaveWhere makes the GitHub repo for you without leaving the app — Repository name (prefilled from the cave name, spaces→hyphens), Visibility PRIVATE (default; "Only you and collaborators can see this repository. Recommended for cave survey data.") or PUBLIC ("Anyone on the internet can view this repository and its survey data."), "Create repository" (a taken name is rejected). Or "Already have a remote? →" to CONNECT an existing repo: a GitHub-repository list, or a Custom URL (https or SSH — SSH needs your key already configured). One remote per project; finishes with "Remote configured. Push your project to GitHub to back it up." → Sync now / Close. Then File → Share… opens the SHARE PROJECT dialog with the link prebuilt — "Copy link" copies it. Link form: https://cavewhere.com/open?repo=<https repo url>.git (works for GitHub-hosted repos; a remote elsewhere disables Copy link). For a PRIVATE repo, "Recipients need repository access for private repositories" — invite them on GitHub first (the dialog's "Invite collaborators" link). The link is a DEEP LINK: on macOS/Windows a browser click hands it straight to the app; on Linux the recipient pastes it into File → Open from Link…. Either way it lands in the recipient's Clone Repository dialog.
- [Sync Your Changes](collaboration/sync-your-changes.md): The SYNC BUTTON sits at the far right of the breadcrumb bar and appears once a project has a remote (upload-cloud icon when it has none — click to set one up). HOVER = tooltip states exactly what a click will do. ONE CLICK does the whole exchange in order: saves+commits your work locally (so even unsaved edits are recorded first), PULLS teammates' changes and MERGES them with the data-model merge, then PUSHES yours up — commits first so nothing is lost if the network step fails. BADGE on the button: "↑N" versions to push, "↓N" to pull, "•" uncommitted local edits, "!" (yellow) = status unavailable/couldn't reach remote (often just offline), "…" = syncing; no badge = up to date. TOOLTIP strings: "Up to date", "Updates available — click to sync", "Commits ready to push — click to sync", "Commits to push and pull — click to sync", "Unsaved changes — click to save and sync", "Local edits pending — click to sync", "Sign in to GitHub — click to sync", "GitHub access expired — click to reconnect". SAVE records a local version; SYNC shares it. On quit/switch with unsaved work + a remote, the save prompt adds SAVE & SYNC (pushes as you leave; if the sync part fails it says "GitHub access has expired. Your changes are saved locally." and lets you close anyway — work is saved either way). VERSION COMPATIBILITY: a project created by a NEWER CaveWhere disables the button with "Sync disabled — upgrade CaveWhere to v…" rather than dropping data. MERGE CONFLICTS: syncing COMBINES everyone's edits automatically — there is NO conflict-resolution UI and no conflict markers. Edits to DIFFERENT things (different trips/shots/notes/caves) always merge; edits to the EXACT SAME field are settled silently in favor of the LOCAL (syncing) copy = yours, and the teammate's value for that one field is not applied to the current map — but their change is NOT lost, it stays as their own save in the history where you can see it and re-enter it. Only bites when two people edit the same reading at the same time. A raw "Merge Conflicts need to be resolved" message is a rarely-reached safety net (appears as a yellow warning, committed work untouched since sync commits first); the fix is to Sync again, or Discard back to your last save. Right-click MENU: Sync now / Set up remote… / Install CaveWhere on GitHub / Remote settings… (the Remote Management page — removing a remote "does not delete the remote repository — it only removes the link from this project") / History… / Log in-out. An expired sign-in raises a Reconnect popup.
- [Open a Shared Project](collaboration/open-a-shared-project.md): CLONE (download a full copy with history) a cave someone shared, or your own online project onto another machine. Two routes. File → OPEN FROM LINK… : paste a share link (https://cavewhere.com/open?repo=…), a cavewhere://open?repo=… link, or a plain repo URL (https://github.com/user/cave.git) and click Open — this is the LINUX route and the route the example cave uses; on macOS/Windows a browser click on the link does it for you. File → OPEN FROM ONLINE… (Ctrl+Shift+O): a page that lists YOUR OWN GitHub repos after you sign in (Public/Private badge + filter) — click one to fill its address then Clone; also has a "Clone from HTTP/SSH" box for pasting a URL. Difference: Open from Link takes one link someone gave you; Open from Online lets you pick from your list. Both reach the CLONE REPOSITORY dialog: names the repo as "host / path" (e.g. github.com / your-org/your-cave), a changeable DESTINATION folder (click the path), an ACCOUNT picker that matters ONLY for a PRIVATE repo (pick the account with access; a PUBLIC repo clones with no sign-in), and Clone & Open with a progress bar. If a private repo returns an access error, CaveWhere shows the sign-in panel and auto-retries; a persistent failure hints the repo doesn't exist for your account or you have an unaccepted collaborator invitation to accept on GitHub. Because CaveWhere holds ONE project at a time, cloning deals with the currently-open project first. The cloned cave lands as a normal editable local project WITH ITS REMOTE ALREADY SET (ready to sync from the first edit) and joins the recent projects list.
- [Review Project History](collaboration/review-history.md): The HISTORY page (right-click the Sync button → "History…"; no sidebar button) lists every saved version NEWEST-FIRST — each row shows the message, author, a short code, and the DATE (day only, yyyy-MM-dd). Every ordinary save is titled "Save from CaveWhere," so the messages are identical; the AUTHOR, DATE, and CHANGED-FILE LIST are what distinguish versions. Branch labels mark LOCAL ("commits saved on your device") vs REMOTE ("the latest commits pushed to the server") — a local label above the remote one is the same story as the Sync button's ↑ badge. Selecting a version shows author/full date/SHA and "N changed file(s)" with a letter per file (A added / M modified / D deleted) and +/- line counts; clicking a file opens a LINE-BY-LINE DIFF that is genuinely readable for survey data because caves/trips/shots are stored as text (note images and point clouds are BINARY and can't be text-diffed). Changed NOTE IMAGES get a side-by-side BEFORE/AFTER compare. A pencil-marked top row = UNCOMMITTED edits → its panel offers "Commit All Changes" (with a custom subject/description, unlike a normal save) or "Discard All" (warns it "cannot be undone"). RESTORE TO HERE (right-click a version) rolls back NON-DESTRUCTIVELY: "This will create a new save that restores the project to… All history will be preserved." — it adds a new version whose contents match the old one and keeps everything in between. REVERTING changes has exactly two forms: DISCARD (back to your LAST save — clears uncommitted edits + untracked files, "cannot be undone", same as the Discard button when quitting/switching) and RESTORE (back to an EARLIER save, non-destructive). Restore is WHOLE-PROJECT only and replaces any unsaved edits; there is NO way IN CAVEWHERE to revert a single version in the middle while keeping later ones, and no per-file restore — but because a .cwproj is a normal Git repository, a Git-comfortable user can do that kind of surgery (e.g. undo one commit) with raw Git on the command line, with the project closed in CaveWhere.

- [Change CaveWhere's Settings](settings/change-settings.md): The app-wide preferences page, opened from File → Settings… (macOS menu bar, or the File button in the sidebar on Windows/Linux). It is a PAGE, not a modal dialog: a vertical tab list (Jobs, Warping, PDF / SVG, Git, Appearance, Rendering) with the selected tab's controls on the right. KEY FRAMING: every setting is stored PER MACHINE alongside the app (QSettings), NOT inside any project — so it applies to every cave you open on this computer, does NOT travel with a project you share, and never edits or risks survey data. There is NO OK / Apply / Cancel (changes apply and save immediately, just close the page); each tab has its own Restore Defaults button (greyed when already at defaults); small "i" buttons reveal in-place help. Two tabs are documented elsewhere because they belong to an earlier task: GIT = your identity (name/email that signs saves; see getting-started/set-up-your-identity.md), WARPING = how sketches morph onto the survey (see scraps/warping-settings.md). The four this page covers: (1) JOBS — "Max Number of Threads" (default = all usable threads; "Usable threads: N" shows the machine's count) caps how many background jobs run at once; the ONLY reason to lower it is HEAT (a laptop running hot / fans spinning) — CaveWhere still does everything, just less in parallel. This is NOT the sidebar's Automatic Update checkbox (which decides whether recompute runs at all; see scraps/carpeting.md). (2) PDF / SVG — a PDF/SVG note is rasterized (turned to pixels) on import; "PDF / SVG rasterization resolution (in pixels per inch)" defaults to 96 ppi (balance of sharpness vs memory), raise up to 600 ppi for a dense drawing you'll zoom into (capped 600 ppi / 256 MB per note), or 72 ppi (PDF) / 92 ppi (SVG) for a 1:1 import; a "PDFs is supported/unsupported" indicator reflects build-time PDF support (official downloads all support it; only a source build lacking Qt's PDF module reports unsupported — SVG doesn't need that module). See notes/add-a-note.md. (3) APPEARANCE — changes the INTERFACE font: Font picks a family (CaveWhere [the app's own display font, default], Fira Sans, or System [the OS font]); Size has Smaller / Default / Larger buttons stepping the base size ±2 (10–28 px, shown in px; the Default card is outlined at the standard size); a live Preview shows headings/UI/body/small text. It's a per-machine look preference, doesn't affect the cave or other people's view. (4) RENDERING — "MSAA samples" (multisample anti-aliasing) smooths jagged edges in the 3D view: Off (1×), 2×, 4×, … ; the list only offers counts the graphics hardware supports (platform dependent), default 4×; more samples look better but cost more GPU per frame, ESPECIALLY with a point cloud in view because Eye-Dome Lighting shading runs once per sample; applies to the 3D view immediately. (The 7th tab, Sketch, is a prototype being removed and is intentionally undocumented.)

## Meta

- [Authoring guide](AUTHORING.md): Conventions for writing manual pages — problem-first structure, front matter, links, and image rules. For contributors, not end users.
- [Manual index](index.md): Human-readable table of contents and chapter map.
